pattern: A breadth-first flood started simultaneously from every land cell labels each water cell with its nearest-land distance, and the last cell reached gives the maximum.
hints: You want, for every water cell, the distance to its closest land cell, then the largest of those distances.
Instead of searching outward from each water cell, push outward from all land cells at once so distances grow level by level.
Seed a queue with every land cell at distance 0, run a multi-source BFS over the four neighbors, and the depth of the final layer is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: single land cell in a corner

stress.description: random grids with mixed land and water
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 30)))
    grid = [[1 if rng.random() < 0.25 else 0 for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(n)]
    return [grid]
stress.note: square grid as required; sparse land keeps distances interesting
